Transported Hessian Transport Rules On Quadric Graphs

  • Purpose
  • Affine Probe Residuals
  • Quadratic And Cubic Probes
  • Affine Versus Quadratic Separation
  • Fractional-Power Probe Panel
  • Row Retention And Matching Diagnostics
  • Results Summary
  • Appendix: Run Status
  • Appendix: Reproduction

Purpose

This report compares the currently implemented transported-Hessian transport families on data-derived graphs sampled from quadratic hypersurfaces. A sample starts from intrinsic coordinates

\[ u=(u_1,\ldots,u_m) \]

and embeds them as a quadric graph

\[ \Phi(u)=\bigl(u,\ q(u)\bigr), \qquad q(u)=\sum_{i\le k} c_i u_i^2-\sum_{i>k} c_i u_i^2. \]

The graph is a connected symmetric kNN graph in the ambient data coordinates \(\Phi(u)\). Every method builds an order-2 transported-Hessian-like operator \(A\). The probes are intrinsic chart functions, so the test asks whether a transport rule behaves like an intrinsic graph second-difference operator.

The method families are:

  • regression-gradient transport, including intrinsic oracle, ambient data coordinates, and PCA;
  • soft coordinate direction matching;
  • the new edge-angle hard and soft rules, which compare directions by their angle relative to the oriented base dart.

The exact-coordinate rule is intentionally excluded: it is a useful grid/path reference, but generic quadric kNN graphs do not have axis-aligned direction labels. The graph-derived local-chart backend for regression-gradient transport is also excluded from this particular grid because it rebuilds a local embedding per dart; that backend is tested in the separate regression-gradient validation report.

Build: 2026-05-19 07:27:37 EDT
Mode: smoke
Runs: 14
Cases: 2
Graph weights: ambient

Affine Probe Residuals

For intrinsic affine probes \(u_i\), the target diagnostic is

\[ R_{\mathrm{aff}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\|A u_i\|_2}{\|u_i\|_2}. \]

A plausible second-difference operator should make this small. This is the main correctness screen for whether a transport rule respects the intrinsic local linear structure of the sampled surface.

Interpretation: regression-gradient with intrinsic or ambient coordinates is the affine benchmark. Methods that stay close to it are successfully preserving the null space expected of a second-difference-like construction. Methods that sit orders of magnitude higher are not reliable affine annihilators on these data-derived graphs.

Quadratic And Cubic Probes

The operator should not annihilate all higher-order structure. For quadratic and cubic intrinsic probes we report

\[ R_{\mathcal F} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal F|} \sum_{p\in\mathcal F} \frac{\|A p\|_2}{\|p\|_2}, \]

where \(\mathcal F\) is either the set of intrinsic monomials \(u_i u_j\) or \(u_i u_j u_k\). Cubic probes are diagnostics only; this report is still testing order-2 operators.

Interpretation: very small affine residual together with nonzero quadratic and cubic residual is the desired qualitative pattern. If a method also drives quadratic residuals to nearly zero, it is too weak for a Hessian-like role.

Affine Versus Quadratic Separation

A useful operator should occupy the lower-right portion of this plot: low affine residual, but appreciable quadratic residual. This plot therefore checks both null-space behavior and signal detection in one view.

Interpretation: this plot makes clear when a method is failing because it does not annihilate affine functions, versus when it is failing because it has become too weak overall.

Fractional-Power Probe Panel

Fractional probes use the intrinsic radius

\[ \rho(u)= \frac{\|u-\bar u\|_2}{\max_w\|w-\bar u\|_2} \]

and evaluate \(\rho^\alpha\) for non-integer \(\alpha\). These are not meant to define a polynomial null space. They are a stress test for whether the operator has a gradual, non-integer response profile rather than an all-or-nothing integer-degree behavior.

Interpretation: a smooth monotone response is reassuring. A method whose fractional response is erratic is likely being dominated by matching ambiguity or row loss rather than by a clean differential analogy.

Row Retention And Matching Diagnostics

For match-based methods, low residuals can be misleading if most candidate rows were dropped. We therefore inspect row retention,

\[ \mathrm{retention} = \frac{\#\{\hbox{accepted rows}\}} {\#\{\hbox{candidate rows}\}}. \]

Interpretation: a method that looks good only after aggressive dropping should be treated as a diagnostic filter, not as a stable operator.

Results Summary

Median diagnostics by method, ranked by affine residual.
method.label method.family residual.affine.mean residual.quadratic.mean residual.cubic.mean row.retention
5 Regression gradient: intrinsic oracle regression.gradient 0.0000 4.2266 4.7309 1.0000
6 Regression gradient: data coordinates regression.gradient 0.0000 5.8556 8.5383 1.0000
7 Regression gradient: PCA chart regression.gradient 1.6128 4.7066 5.2231 1.0000
4 Soft direction matching: gated local.embedding.soft 4.5771 7.8829 8.7455 0.6747
3 Soft direction matching local.embedding.soft 8.1953 12.1420 13.7673 1.0000
1 Edge-angle hard matching edge.angle 8.7748 10.8719 11.9004 0.7987
2 Edge-angle soft matching edge.angle 9.7418 12.7760 14.3643 1.0000

The lowest median affine residual in this run is produced by Regression gradient: intrinsic oracle. The preferred transported-Hessian behavior is not only low affine residual, but also nonzero quadratic/cubic residual and healthy row retention. The edge-angle methods are intentionally included here as a new baseline: they test how far one can get from local angle matching alone, before moving to richer direction-transport rules.

Appendix: Reproduction

# Smoke render
Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_transported_hessian_quadric_multimethod_validation.R

# Full render
GFLOW_QUADRIC_MULTIMETHOD_VALIDATION_MODE=full \
  Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_transported_hessian_quadric_multimethod_validation.R
